statement: Please do this because I'm Latin American, so do that
Well, the excellencies and dear friends, my candidacy for Secretary General of the United Nations is grounded on one clear conviction
We must build the United Nations the world needs
A UN that not merely administered the present, but one with the capacity to transform it
An organization that's more effective
more transparent, closer to the people, and capable of delivering concrete results
My own vision was born of a difficult and sometimes improbable miracle of dialogue, even though when all odds were against it, I have seen it transform the impossible into the achievable
Trust in the international order and trust in our institutions are mutually reinforcing
both require robust experience and firm commitment to the short term

I stand before you to reclaim the urgent need for dialogue because deliberation and multilateral cooperation have been the operative platform upon which this organization was built and they remain the bridge to our future
I believe in a nation that looks in four directions at once
First, backwards
It remains true to the principles of the Charter
I think the principles of the Charter are the moral and political compass of the international system
Any reform must serve to better realise that founding vision
Second, inward, to modernise and strengthen the institution
with transparency, efficiency and accountability, eliminating redundancies while eliminating field capacities, and turning reform from an episodic effort into continuous day-to-day commitment
Third, if I said backward, inward, third is outward
to reconnect with people
The UN's legitimacy depends on its ability to deliver concrete results
Because if people do not see the UN working, they will not believe in multilateralism and forward to collectively design the future
The Centennial of 2045 is a working horizon

We must design the UN of the future today, with gender parity in the Secretariat, with real representation from all regions, and with the three pillars – peace and security, development and human rights – properly financed and balanced
The United Nations was stressed on three deeply interconnected pillars, peace and security, sustainable development and human rights
On peace and security, prevention must lie at the heart of our action
Quiet diplomacy, early warning, sustained engagement with real actors, and professional mediation are always more effective than reacting after a crisis already erupted
The Secretary General must remain an active channel of communication even when consensus seems distant and must be present also in the field
On sustainable development, our priorities must be to eradicate hunger and poverty

reduce inequalities, close the digital divide, and advance meaningful climate action, among other things, so that we may leave young generations a world that offers them opportunity, dignity, and hope
This also requires a genuine reform of the international financial architecture, so that the most vulnerable countries are not left behind
On human rights, human dignity must remain at the center of everything the UN does
Human rights are not only the foundation of a life lived in dignity, they are also a preventive instrument for last peace and global stability

And central to this is the empowerment of women, girls and youth, whose full and meaningful participation in peace processes and decision-making is essential to building more just and resilient countries and societies

I think that one of the unique elements of the United Nations is that it's the only irreplaceable space where humanity comes together to overcome common challenges in a world fragmented by disinformation, polarization, and competition of power
The UN must be a place where difference becomes consensus, wars outweigh violence, and where collective decisions reflect a shared responsibility to preserve peace and human dignity
The 21st century demands a UN
that anticipates, that prevents, that unites
I am ready to lead that effort with experience, commitment and the deep conviction that multilateralism with the world changes lives
Well, I think my skill is not only being at the UN
It's the whole history of my life
Because my personal political trajectory has deeply changed myself
Having been a young girl, when the Chicup d'etat happened in my country, my father, who really believed in rule of law, was put into prison and died by torture
Me and my mom were also in prison and went to exile
And then I learned what happens when institutions fail
when some people take power and don't respect the rule of law
And then after we've been in exile, I learned the importance of multilateralism because I saw solidarity with the people who were suffering
And I think not only because of the help of countries, external countries, of course the battle for democracy in our own country, but so far for important countries, help Chile go back to democracy

When I was elected president of Chile, the first woman president of my country, I also served twice, and also served as cabinet member, as health minister and defense minister, also the first in those positions
And as you know, as the first executive director of UN Women and afterwards as High Commissioner of Human Rights,
In 20 years of my public life, both in national and international, I have combined the fundamental principles with realistic practical leadership
But I have been in places where I have to deal with very complicated situations
Yeah
And I have been able to do that
And also, another thing I think it gives me how the way I act is that I'm a physician, a pediatrician, and epidemiologist
So I'm not only looking at the symptoms
I'm looking at the root causes
Because if you don't understand what's there, you won't find the real solution
And just one example
Many countries have been there
every weekend fights, killings
And it was not, there was different ethnic group, but it was not a matter of identity
It was a matter of lack of water and soil
So if you want to find the solutions to those and prevent conflict happening, you need to understand really
And for that, you need to be on the ground
I have to add one
is only one mandate to be elected
That's also the one for 8 billion
I could subscribe it now
I could describe it right now
All of them
All of them, because that's what I've tried to do throughout my whole life
That's what I believe in
And I believe that you have to make the best decisions with independence
We have to appoint the best possible person
I mean, there's always going to be pressures
But I mean, in my life, I've shown that pressure doesn't
I make decisions because I believe
I was on the hearing at the General Assembly very happily, three hours
It was long, but it was really important because Member States could listen to what I was thinking about many issues
And I'm looking forward if there are hearings at the Security Council soon
And I've been trying to
have activities with civil society in this process
So I think for the fifth principle that you know, for more than two years, I'm not part of the UN
So I have no conflict of interest on that
Well, you know, I think you can do a lot
You can do a lot as Secretary General
First of all, I mentioned the three key tools and
roles of the UN because I believe that when some countries say we need to go back to basics and think that peace and security is the only thing, I don't agree with them
I think you won't have peace and security if you don't have development and you violate human rights
So we need the three of them
And I will do my best to insist that the three of them needs to be supported, funded, et cetera
The second thing is I think the charter has a lot of tools available, early warning, good offices, mediation
And I am a person who's not waiting to do those things
If I realized that a conflict will come, I will try to go to anticipate myself to go before to the field
And if it's not able to prevent it, to work on mediation, to use our good offices
I mean, sometimes you try things and they don't work
But I think you have to insist, Brian

Well, what I'll say, if there are many different reasons, because there has been sort of a tendency of sort of not supporting multilateral solution and supporting unilateral solution or mid-lab multilateral solutions

And I believe that there are always situations that group of countries can help, of course, that's fine, it's always been like that, or they can mediate, but it has to be under the coordination and coherence with the United Nations, with the Security Council
But I do believe if I'm a Secretary General, I will go there to the field
I won't wait until they invite me to go
I will go there
If it's necessary, I'll be in front of the building of the Ministry for Foreign Affairs
Because I believe the Secretary General is the, if I would say, the more important diplomat in the world
And he has to be a moral voice
So I will speak, but I will go to the field
Because there is discussion
Should I be a secretary or a general
You'll be more general, given your defense
I mean, you have to be the chief administrator, according to the charter
But I have to be a general as well
Well, I remember when I was at UN Women, there was a group working with girls, and they said, I don't want to be at the table
I want to decide the shape of the table
And I understand that's the same thing that these people are saying
I believe, first of all, there's a lot that can be done with EcoSoc
Because ECOSOC is the one who accredits which are NGOs, civil society organizations that can be observers, that can participate
So that can be, I would say, improved
All those procedures can be improved because it takes too long and that means that you have to ensure that more civil society organizations and NGOs can be present at the different places
We need, though, I have to say, to ensure that they get visas
so they can participate in many of these because I saw in the CSW very few women from some parts of the world and I asked what was it
They said the cost of the ticket
No, they said they didn't get the visa
So we need to ensure that that happens as well
We need to enforce the Youth Office
We need to support the Youth Office so it can make much more than what it's doing now
So I believe that also we have the solution 2250 that on youth and peace and security
And I think we need to make it a reality because that is written, but you have to implement it
Well, everybody who knows me knows that I've been my whole life pushing for gender equality, for women's empowerment
I'm continuing to do so as Secretary General or not as Secretary General
So I think we need to continue
building upon what Secretary-General Guterres has done, because he has ensured that in all senior positions, women and men are parity, 50-50
But that has to go down as well
But also in the Secretariat, you have to have better representation, gender representation, geographical representation, et cetera
We also need to continue speaking out loud about women's rights and to ensure that we don't have a bigger backlash
I saw that backlash when I was new in women
I will continue doing that job
We need to build alliances with governments to push for gender rights
And the other question, and the same with civic space
We need people being part of the solution
I mean, even in our common agenda, we said that we need to redefine what the international community was
It was not only about intergovernmental, it needed also to have all these other activists, civil society, academia, private sector, et cetera
So that we need to continue opening these six spaces and trying also to reach out member states to continue doing so
I would engage
If I'm Secretary General, I have to maintain climate change in the agenda because some countries are still saying that climate change doesn't exist
Some countries are discussing about going back to coal and other fossil combustibles and I think that's very dangerous
So we need first of all to continue showing science with evidence why climate change is a reality and we need to maintain
And then we need to think on how we finance it because climate action needs support
And today, you know, the countries who have less contributed to the climate change, to the problems, are the ones who receive less money

So we need to think of innovative mechanisms, for example, like debt climate swaps or other mechanisms that can ensure that countries who are not the responsible ones can really have the resources to do their mitigation and adaptation plans and so on

Well, I think that one of the main tasks that the UN has to ensure is to protect lives and protect lives not only trying to avoid conflicts but also to deal with the results not only on conflict but because you need a military assistance also when you have diseases, when you have
natural disasters and so on

So I believe that humanitarian assistance is key, but it has to be also related to the other areas that we need to work on because of course we have to provide humanitarian assistance when things are happening, but as I said before,
we need to prevent them from happening because probably there will never be enough resources to solve all those problems
And prevention is essentially to avoid not only the losses of life, the situation you mentioned, but also the cost that it means

But on the other hand, I believe there is a huge group of people with a lot of experience that have done fantastic jobs on humanitarian assistance and I think we need to continue supporting them strongly
Yeah
I know that the UNED reform is mainly being pushed because of cost cutting and because of lack of liquidity
But if you have savings making all those initiatives that the reform intends to do, avoiding the redundancy and so on,
That doesn't – that needs to be put, those money, in the areas that you prioritize
Again, I will look like somebody who's repeating, but human rights, development, and peace and security
Because the thing of the reform is not to do more with less, it's to do better and put the money where the priorities are
Because you believe the critics who say it is too bloated, you do need to slim it down
No, I do believe that the UN has to be leaner,
but muscle, not leaner but weak

So one of the things that I believe and I will push strongly is that it can be a reform, it can be, it must be a reform, it's an opportunity as well, but what cannot happen is to diminish the field capacity
And it cannot happen not to do what it needs to do because otherwise we're losing the essentials that make this new organization exist
Well, I'm not sure if I'm that master
Because when things are very complicated, you need to be there energetically
The only thing that I want to say is that for me to be a secretary-general is not to be the glory of to be the first women secretary-general
I have many glories in my life and I don't need more
So what is your hobby
You've got five seconds
My hobby
Read, dance
But one thing I said, I would like to be a secretary-general if it's worthy
If I can be really independent, neutral, and serve all member states
That is what I would like
